title: Supporting Details
description: Assessment of the concept that strong supporting details are relevant, specific, and directly connected to the central idea.

questions.prompt: Which description best fits a strong supporting detail?

choices.text: A statement that is relevant, specific, and connected to the central idea
choices.feedback: This is correct because strong supporting details clearly connect to and develop the central idea.

choices.text: A sentence that sounds impressive even if it does not relate to the topic
choices.feedback: A supporting detail must be relevant to the central idea, not just interesting.

choices.text: Any fact that appears in the text, no matter its purpose
choices.feedback: Not every fact is a strong supporting detail. It must directly support the main idea.

choices.text: A reader's personal reaction to the topic
choices.feedback: Personal reaction is not textual support for a central idea.

questions.prompt: A passage argues that the school garden improves the campus. Which detail best supports that central idea?

choices.text: The garden fence is painted green.
choices.feedback: This detail may be true, but it does not strongly show how the garden improves the campus.

choices.text: Students use the garden to grow vegetables for the cafeteria and to learn science skills.
choices.feedback: This is correct because it specifically shows meaningful ways the garden benefits the school.
This is too personal and does not directly support the broader claim about campus improvement.

choices.text: The article begins with a question about spring weather.
choices.feedback: This may describe the writing, but it does not function as strong support for the central idea.

questions.prompt: Why is the detail "The club meets every Thursday at 3:15" weak support for the central idea "The club helps students become stronger writers"?

choices.text: It gives a schedule but does not show how the club improves writing
choices.feedback: This is correct because a supporting detail must connect directly to the central idea, not just provide unrelated information.

choices.text: It is too precise to belong in an informational text
choices.feedback: Specificity is not the problem. The issue is lack of connection to the central idea.

choices.text: It should be rewritten as a question instead of a statement
choices.feedback: Changing the sentence form would not solve the real problem, which is weak relevance.

choices.text: It uses a number, and numbers should not appear in details
choices.feedback: Numbers can be useful supporting details when they actually support the main point.

questions.prompt: Which detail would best support the central idea "After-school tutoring increases student confidence"?

choices.feedback: This detail gives location, but it does not show an increase in confidence.

choices.text: Students in the program reported feeling more prepared to participate in class discussions.
choices.feedback: This is correct because it directly shows a result connected to increased confidence.

choices.text: The tutor uses blue folders for assignments.
choices.feedback: This detail is too minor and unrelated to the central idea about confidence.

choices.text: Some students arrived five minutes early.
choices.feedback: This detail does not clearly support the claim about confidence.

questions.prompt: True or False: A supporting detail is strong only if it directly helps prove or explain the central idea.

choices.feedback: This is correct because strong supporting details must clearly connect to the text's central idea.
This is incorrect because a detail that does not help explain or prove the central idea is weak support.
